speaker: interviewer
text: You were telling me that after your mother was taken away, your father lost interest in eating, and you were worried about him.

speaker: interviewee
text: Interest in living. He could not work anymore because he couldn't walk. And the moment he was falling over, he was beaten. And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] the fact that they have taken my father-- my mother away, my father and myself were completely destroyed. Father lost interest in living, and I was, without speaking to him, convinced that Mother went to Auschwitz. And that was terrible blow to both of us. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] We did know a doctor in this-- there was a sort of hospital, a place where one would go when one had some injury. His name was Dr. Lefkowitch, whom we knew. Because when we were in the ghetto, we lived in the same house. You know, each time the transport went away, the ghetto was made smaller. And on the second occasion, we lived in the house with a family called [INAUDIBLE], to whose son my sister was really engaged. And next door, Dr. Lefkowitch used to live. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And Mrs. Gross, whose husband also was a doctor-- that famous Dr. Gross, from Plaszow. So I went and I begged they should take my father to hospital to give him chance to recuperate, to revive him. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] It was no revival. They have taken him to hospital, to isolation hospital. And the following morning when I came from night duty, I wanted to see my father and I brought him a little cup with boiled potatoes. Nobody wanted to come to the gate to pick the potatoes from me to take to my father. And you know, you're sort of intuitive, really. You realize something is not quite right. Eventually I stood there and waited and waited. And two fellows, two men, came with a body on a stretcher. They stopped, they put the body down, and I raised the blanket. And I have seen that that was my father's body. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] That was the following day after he went to the hospital to recuperate. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I didn't cry. There was no performance. I stood there for a while. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I saw that they were carrying him to a place where they were burning bodies up the hill. And I saw the smoke later. And a lady with whom I have been friendly with after my mother was taken away told me, remember, today is one day before Erev Purim. If you survive, you have to say kaddish. It's the memorial prayer for the dead. I accepted. I accepted. If anything, it was a sense of relief. They killed my mother. They took her to Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you thought.

speaker: interviewee
text: My father is dead. Now I have nothing to worry about. All I need to finish with myself somehow, some way. And this is it. And I became very reckless. I didn't go to work. I didn't go to Appelplatz. I tried to avoid doing what they tell-- what they were telling us to do. I had nothing to lose anymore.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did your father died of?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father died. He was given an injection of naphtha. And that I have found out after the war that it was naphtha. Very recently, like two weeks ago, there was a girl here from Melbourne. And we started to talk about it. We lapsed again into concentration camp. And she said, my uncle was working, the Dr. Leff-- he was not the same one Dr. Lefkowitch-- and he knew that they were giving naphtha. So I knew that Father did not die of natural causes. But at that stage, I thought probably he did because he didn't want to eat. He refused to eat. He was smoking cigarettes. You know, one could get cigarettes in the camp. How and who brought it, I don't know. Probably the SS or the Army soldiers or whoever was making a business, black marketeering. And Father would give them bread and got a cigarette.

speaker: interviewee
text: So [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I knew that-- then that we are there just to be-- for destruction. It's no way of getting out of it. It didn't matter anymore. And to some extent, that probably helped me to survive because I didn't-- I didn't wear myself out getting up 5:00 in the morning to go to work, coming back late from work.

speaker: interviewee
text: You do had-- you had to work. You were supervised all the time. The food was meager. So I didn't go to work. For quite a while, I was looking for Blockalteste who would want me to scrub the floor. And for that, she would give me bread and soup to eat.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the Blockalteste? Who were they?

speaker: interviewee
text: Blockalteste-- in charge of the block. They were usually women who had some sort of a [INAUDIBLE], favors. Or they knew somebody, because that was a good job to have. But they were reluctant to wash the floors themselves. So they were glad to have me. And I was glad to do it, because I did get something to eat. And so I would hide myself. And there was constantly searchers in the block that nobody stayed behind, that everybody went to work. They would come, the-- the policemen, and look with a bayonet, SS, looking if anybody was hidden. But on the rare occasions that I encountered that somebody's bayonet was near me. And as the block-- as the beds were-- you know, about 500 people to a block. So it was a long thing like three tiers of beds, this way and this way.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I would normally lie lengthwise that way so they couldn't retrieve me. I was very long. If I lie a different way, my legs would be sticking out. What would have happened if they'd found you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I'm sure beaten up to a pulp or shot or whatever. There was no question about it. You just did not do anything that they didn't allow you. You thought that if you subordinate, you survive. But I knew better. I knew better. I could see that people were subordinating and they did not survive. My father was subordinating. He was going to work. He tried his utmost until he couldn't anymore. Until-- And each time he was flogged. And I had to watch that-- coming back from work, bleeding from this leg, from that leg, from body. He would never complain, but I could see what they had done to him-- a man of 50. There just was no way of surviving it. And lots of other people. One day, my father was not there anymore. My mother was not there anymore. I was-- I ventured into the camp, out of that block where I was scrubbing and hiding. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It was in the morning. Probably I was going to that potato-peeling place to get some food. And I saw a boy crossing from bakery towards the block where the policemen were. And he was carrying a brown bag. That size of a bag. It could have had 10 bread rolls there, which he did.

speaker: interviewee
text: An SS, [INAUDIBLE], came across, called him. What have you got today? And he was slightly retarded. I knew him well. We used to call him [NON-ENGLISH] kasha, because he-- whatever you asked him, what would you like to eat? He would say, [NON-ENGLISH] kasha. Only kasha he liked. So that's what he was called.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he was used as a courier between the police and the bakery. He pulled out revolver-- [INAUDIBLE], SS [INAUDIBLE]-- at a short distance, like from me to you, and he shot him. The bread rolls fell down. [NON-ENGLISH] kasha fell, and I continued to walk. I pretended I'm not looking, even, because I knew if I looked, I will be the next. That's how we were trained. That's-- my-- that was my observation. No hysteria, no crying, no yelling out, somebody got shot! There was a young boy lying there. That were the experiences in Plaszow-- some of them. And there were much worse-- much, much worse. And that was still right at the beginning. I want to tell you the story of hanging. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] There was one Sunday afternoon. We were all called to Appelplatz. My mother was still there. My father was still in the camp. So we would go. My mother was also very correct. If they're calling, we have to go. We have to go. And we stood there, the whole 10,000 of us or more. That was the amount of people living at that stage in the camp. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We came to Appelplatz. We were counted make sure nobody was missing. There were gallows standing, and 10 tables. Wooden 10 tables through the length of our standing there. 10 people to each table were selected from that group of the 10,000, surrounded with SS with guns. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] But beforehand, [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] there was few people to be hanged-- four. Two people I knew very well. One was engineer Krautfeer, and the other boy's name was Helbenshtock, young boy. And we were told, you know, told by somebody that they were running away from the camp. So that's why they were caught and they're going to be hanged. And that was to scare us never to try to run away, because whoever did-- we did know that people did try to run away-- and they were shot. And on this occasion, they said that every-- out of every 100 persons, one is going to be shot every-- so [INAUDIBLE] sure enough, the people-- one of them, that engineer Krautfeer, already committed suicide beforehand. He cut his wrists. Dead as he was, he was hanged. The other two people were standing there waiting to be hanged. There was another boy, Helbenshtock, to be hanged, and he was being hanged by somebody with-- who was trying desperately to save his life. And he fell once, twice, three times. Because they thought that if he will fall, his life will be spared, according to the Geneva convention. His life was not only not spared. But Goeth pulled out his gun, and he said to the man who was hanging him, if you won't hang him, you will be hanged and I shoot you here.

speaker: interviewer
text: Amon Goeth said that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Amon Goeth. Oh, it was Amon Goeth in every performance. He was taking the main part in everything. [SIGHS] The boy was hanged-- young boy, 17 years old. And the other people were hanged. Then they started to beat us. I was amongst the people chosen to be beaten. [BACKGROUND NOISE] I don't know who it is. The beaten-- the beating was consisting of [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] taking knickers down, lying with bare bottom on the table, everybody watching. And you had to count 25 lashes--

speaker: interviewer
text: While they were hitting you?

speaker: interviewee
text: While they were hitting me, you had to count. And God forbid if you stop counting, or if you scream, you got more. [BACKGROUND NOISE]

speaker: interviewee
text: I think it's somebody at that door. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And-- So what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was--

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you able to keep counting?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I was able to count. I don't know how-- probably like, per usual, five, 10, 15, 20, 25, and I got away with it. Jumped off the table, pulled my knickers up, and I stood there for the next-- for the next performance-- part of the performance.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they hit very hard?

speaker: interviewee
text: They hit very hard. The lashes were leaving broken skin and one was beating-- one was bleeding. There was a fellow who fainted. So they very quickly took the opportunity to get bucket of water. And Goeth called out in German to bring a bucket of water. I ran around to the block which was nearby in the hope that maybe I'll get out of that beating, out of that shooting after. We all knew what's happening to us. But unfortunately, I couldn't. Something happened that I had to go back quick-- the bucket-- somebody else was hiding in the barrack. And they said, no, no, no! You can't hide here! Take the water and run.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I took the water and I ran back to the [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] performance, to the place where the tables were standing, where the people were beaten, 100 of us. And at one point, as the beating was being carried out, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] somebody arrived-- another SS, tall, elegant, well-dressed. And only at the end of the war, recently, I have found out that that was Schindler. Oskar Schindler?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oskar Schindler.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was he there for?

speaker: interviewee
text: He came to tell to Amon Goeth, leave the lot. Leave the [GERMAN]. Leave the [GERMAN], in German.

speaker: interviewer
text: What does that mean?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the [INAUDIBLE], the nobodies. They-- it's a swear word he used, but swear words which were very often used in German language. I brought somebody downstairs and you will have a good time. Leave them. Come with me downstairs.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you understand that to mean?

speaker: interviewee
text: That he brought a women to enjoy, to have a pleasant afternoon. And at that point, I thought, we can't even in peace be shot and get on with it. Sort of-- not to procrastinate. I had no idea that we will be allowed to go back to block. I thought we would stand there, as often did stand-- as often did happen, that people had to stand for hours to be shot or to be hanged. It wasn't just that easy that they let you die. And I resented it.

speaker: interviewee
text: But somehow they have disbanded us, and we were allowed to go back to block. And my mother asked me, did it hurt? Did they hurt you very much? Show me.

speaker: interviewee
text: I said, no. They didn't hurt me. And I hit my bottom to show her, no, it didn't hurt. It did hurt. I had knickers sticking to my bottom. To that extent, it did hurt.

speaker: interviewer
text: But Oskar Schindler's presence that day--

speaker: interviewee
text: --Indirectly saved that 100 people's lives.

speaker: interviewer
text: Including yours.

speaker: interviewee
text: Including mine. And that I have only found out when there was an interview by Tom Keneally when he was writing the book from other people that that's what Oskar Schindler did. Somehow he must have been notified by-- from the Jewish headquarters-- from the office where the policeman were-- probably about people like [INAUDIBLE] and Dr. Greenberg, who was also in that group of 100. I don't know who else was that was family or somebody that they wanted to save. They sent for Schindler. Come and save that 100 because they're all going to be shot. And Schindler realized and used that excuse-- the women-- to get Amon Goeth down. And Amon Goeth went down, and we were not shot that day. Today I realized that it was Schindler, not-- knowing then who Schindler was. I knew about Emalia. I knew that there was a working place in Zablocie, out of ghetto, that if you had certain amount of money you could get in and you could work and be saved.

speaker: interviewee
text: But somehow we never had sufficient money to get in there. And we felt secure in the house where we lived because we had somebody there who would tell us when there is transport to go. You know? It sort of gave a certain sense of false security. Because if the SS would have walked into that house, we would have been finished, too. How does a girl of 16 cope with been whipped publicly like that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very humiliating. Dehumanizing. For life-- not because the SS have seen my bottom, but because of the rest of the Jews, the people who stood there, amongst them there were young boys whom I knew from before. And to be hit, to take off the knickers and to be whipped on bare bottom-- it was a hell of a lot to live with after. And-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] well, that wasn't the first time in the last time. Goeth had a special liking for certain people. I think he always picked tall girls [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] to-- to whip. I wouldn't be at all surprised if that was a sexual perversion. I know I haven't been good-looking. I was thin like a rake. There was no notion in my head that he wanted anything else. But that probably gave him satisfaction. He did have a girlfriend in the camp, a beautiful-looking woman. Tall, elegant, riding horses. She had a special beautiful white horse. We saw her often in the camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: We didn't know. I don't know now. I don't know. I have watched an interview with her after the war, and I have seen what has become of her. I almost have felt sorry for her. Because she never did anything out of the ordinary, not like the SS women. Some of them that they were whipping us, hitting us.

speaker: interviewee
text: No. She was very decent-- avoiding us, you know? Sort of all the Germans behaved that way as Amon Goeth, being sadistic and whipping and shooting. And that was every-day occurrence. And for her not to mix with us, not to take that satisfaction that the Jews are being beaten and belittled-- she didn't come to watch that. Well, that was nice. That was-- you know, one had the personal thoughts, and that occurred to me-- such a beautiful woman has all the power. How can she sleep with him?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was enormous. He was absolute terror in the camp. Just the notion that he started to walk out of his office-- it spread like live fire at the camp and we were all hung-- all hiding. Nobody dared to face him. Nobody dared to be out if they didn't have to be. If we were working outside, we were working with our heads down-- well down, so he wouldn't notice us. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So that was Goeth, Amon Goeth. You know, I was terrified of him even when I heard that he has been caught, and he was being prosecuted in Cracow for his deeds-- misdeeds. I was shaking. It was Amon Goeth. I was still shaking, and I was in another country. I was in Austria at that stage, and Amon Goeth was being hanged. It didn't give me satisfaction. I was still terrorized. I was still terrified of it. I didn't lose that fear of him. Even today when I looked at that picture, I thought to myself, that's Amon Goeth. It-- it just-- terrible.

speaker: interviewer
text: It stays with you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: It stays with you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. It still does. It still does. You know, I have a phobia of uniforms. It happened here in Australia. I was very upset. Because my husband was very ill. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And I drove in Kings Cross and there was a policeman standing. At that stage, there was-- in Kings Cross, there was a-- like a little round stand, and he was directing the traffic. There isn't anymore. And I had an old car. My husband said, now you have to learn to drive. So we bought an old Ford for $100. And that car-- it went where it wanted to, not-- and I had just learned to drive.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I wanted to turn from King's Cross, from Victoria Road, where that fire brigade is. What is that street? I think that's a continuation of Victoria Road going into Darling. Because I wanted to cut to Prince of Wales Hospital, to go to my husband.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I saw a car at the back of me-- little Mini-- with two policeman sitting. So I said, because I don't know how to get out from that roundabout, I'll get out of my car and I'll nicely asked those two policemen, as shaking as I was, how to get out. That way I won't do anything wrong.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I went out of the car. I left it in a drive, and the car just started to roll back on its own. And the two policeman behind me pointed out that the car is rolling back. I didn't know what they were pointing out-- probably for me to go back into the car. And I said, yes, yes. But I want to ask you something first.

speaker: interviewee
text: Sure enough, before I even reached him, the car wham into that Mini car, wrecked their lights. And they took me to the station, Victoria Station. When I got to the station, now, first they said to me to pull to the corner. I didn't want to get into that car again.

speaker: interviewee
text: So a policeman pulled the car to the corner. They took me to the station. And I told them, I'm terrified of police. I was in concentration camp. That is a story of 27 years ago. Can we just go back to Plaszow?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you have any other strong memories Plaszow?

speaker: interviewee
text: A lot of strong memories. A lot of strong memories. I'm every day in Plaszow, every day in concentration camp. The slightest things revives the memory. You can never leave the camp. You never leave the Germans. You never leave the-- the horror of it all.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you involved in any selections there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yes. Yes. We were involved. I was involved in selection in Plaszow. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Again, there was no way of hiding. We were called on Sunday to Appelplatz. We had to undress, and they were looking through, walking in between us. Who has not been still fairly well-padded was taken out on the side. And women whose breasts were already flat and hanging were taken out of the rows of people and put aside. Lots of people were taken away, and they sure did go to Auschwitz for extermination. Luckily I was left alone. But of-- one of the Blockalteste, whose daughter was young girl-- her name was Lillian Ingber. And the Blockalteste was Lehrer. It must have been second marriage, because Lilke Ingber was her daughter. Or maybe she was her niece, I'm not totally sure. Look, it's 50 years ago. All I know that Blockalteste felt so sure that, because she's Blockalteste and she was already going back to the block-- they were organizing us-- she went through back to see if that girl is taken to live, that she was not taken to Auschwitz. And they caught her. One SS took her.

speaker: interviewer
text: The Alteste?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. And into the Auschwitz. She didn't survive. She went to Auschwitz while the young girl, to this day, is alive, lives in Canada. You know, moment like that, it just-- that lives with me. Lots of moments, horrifying, live with me.

speaker: interviewer
text: We're just going to stop and change the tape.
